highlights.label: Unified dataset
highlights.note: Every method is evaluated against the same exact-PMID relevance set.

highlights.label: Most precise
highlights.note: Strong term matching stays highly competitive on biomedical phrasing.

highlights.label: Most balanced
highlights.note: Hybrid narrows the gap between lexical precision and semantic breadth.

rows.strength: Transparent lexical baseline with broad coverage.
rows.tradeoff: Less robust when biomedical synonyms diverge from the query string.

rows.strength: Consistently precise when key biomedical terms appear verbatim.
rows.tradeoff: Can stay narrow when concept wording drifts.
rows.bestFor: Exact terminology, focused clinical phrasing, and strong first-pass ranking.

rows.strength: Helps when semantic similarity matters more than word overlap.
rows.tradeoff: Lower precision in this project when dense relevance neighborhoods get too loose.
rows.bestFor: Terminology mismatch, exploratory browsing, and semantic recall.

rows.strength: Combines lexical control with semantic flexibility.
rows.tradeoff: More moving parts and more score calibration work.
rows.bestFor: Balanced retrieval, query comparison, and robust exploration.

clusters.label: Treatment Methods
documents: Metformin intensification strategies across type 2 diabetes cohorts.
Comparative adherence outcomes after escalating oral therapy.
Real-world A1c reduction after guideline-driven treatment changes.

clusters.label: Clinical Outcomes
documents: Cardiovascular endpoint analysis for glucose-lowering therapies.
Weight-sensitive treatment selection in metabolic disease management.
Outcome tradeoffs in high-risk diabetes populations.

clusters.label: Side Effects
documents: Renal safety signals in second-line diabetes pharmacotherapy.
Safety monitoring patterns in multi-drug diabetes care.

clusters.label: Trial Design
documents: Multicenter design considerations in diabetes intervention trials.
Outcome reporting differences across randomized glucose-lowering studies.
Follow-up duration and endpoint quality in metabolic trials.

clusters.label: Biomarkers
documents: Inflammatory biomarker panels associated with treatment response.
Proteomic predictors of glycemic control after therapy escalation.
Insulin-resistance markers in precision diabetes management.

takeaways.title: Best overall
takeaways.body: BM25 leads the current evaluation run, especially on exact biomedical wording and focused clinical questions.

takeaways.title: Where lexical wins
takeaways.body: TF-IDF and BM25 remain strongest when query terms align closely with article titles, abstracts, and specialist terminology.

takeaways.title: Where dense and hybrid help
takeaways.body: Dense retrieval contributes when concept wording drifts, while hybrid preserves better precision by anchoring semantic search to lexical evidence.

queryBuckets.note: Verbatim biomedical phrasing still gives BM25 the cleanest advantage.
Hybrid absorbs terminology mismatch without losing too much lexical precision.
Clinical intervention wording remains well served by lexical ranking.
Hybrid is the safer choice when broader evidence neighborhoods matter.

examples.bestNote: Literal clinical phrasing aligned cleanly with titles and abstracts.
examples.worstNote: Broader phrasing scattered lexical matches across multiple subtopics.

examples.bestNote: Specialist terminology produced a precise and well-ranked result list.
examples.worstNote: Broader intent reduced the advantage of exact term matching.

examples.bestNote: Semantic similarity helped recover safety-oriented papers with looser wording.
examples.worstNote: The semantic neighborhood became too broad compared with the lexical target.

examples.bestNote: Hybrid kept the strongest lexical documents while preserving topical breadth.
examples.worstNote: Sparse terminology and niche phrasing still make this a harder case.

diversitySummary.title: BM25 is the strongest relevance baseline, while Hybrid provides the best relevance-diversity balance.
diversitySummary.body: Across the unified dataset, BM25 stays slightly narrower but more precise, TF-IDF is broadest, Dense is semantically broader than exact term matching but less precise in this run, and Hybrid preserves much of BM25's ranking strength while covering a wider set of biomedical themes.

principles: Course substance comes first: the UI exists to expose retrieval behavior rather than hide it behind product polish.
The corpus is intentionally practical: a curated PubMed subset with PMIDs chosen for local experimentation, indexing, and evaluation.
The academic story stays explicit from end to end: BioASQ questions, exact PMID joins, retrieval comparison, clustering, and diversity analysis.
pipeline: Load BioASQ Task B questions and relevance judgments.
Build a curated PubMed subset with relevant PMIDs plus distractor documents.
Join the two sources using exact PMIDs only.
Index the unified and exploration corpora for lexical and dense retrieval.
